steps.title: Identify the contrast signal
steps.content: The passage presents one theory of bipedalism — it evolved from a ground-based knuckle-walking ancestor — then introduces orangutan research with “But recently.” The word “But” signals that the orangutan evidence points toward a different, tree-based explanation. The blank must complete a claim that follows logically from orangutans standing on two legs in trees while reaching for fruit.

steps.title: Eliminate each choice
steps.content:
• "bipedalism evolved because it was advantageous to a tree-dwelling ancestor of humans.": orangutans standing bipedally in trees to reach fruit directly suggests a tree-dwelling ancestor benefited from upright movement — a clean alternative to the ground-based theory. ✓
  • "bipedalism must have evolved simultaneously with knuckle-walking and tree-climbing.": the orangutan observation gives no information about timing or whether multiple behaviors arose at once; this goes beyond what the evidence supports. ✗
  • "moving between the ground and the trees would have been difficult without bipedalism.": the passage does not address how hard it was to move between ground and trees; this is not implied by the orangutan finding. ✗
  • "a knuckle-walking human ancestor could have easily moved bipedally in trees.": the evidence concerns orangutans, not knuckle-walkers, and says nothing about how easily any ancestor could move bipedally in trees. ✗
That matches the correct answer — A is correct.
